From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Pain medication of the opiate family

This article is about the chemical compound. For other uses, see [morphine (disambiguation)](/wiki/Morphine_(disambiguation) "Morphine (disambiguation)").

"Morphia" redirects here. For other uses, see [Morphia (disambiguation)](/wiki/Morphia_(disambiguation) "Morphia (disambiguation)").

Not to be confused with [morpheein](/wiki/Morpheein "Morpheein") or [morpheme](/wiki/Morpheme "Morpheme").

**Morphine**, formerly known as **morphium**, is an [opiate](/wiki/Opiate "Opiate") found naturally in [opium](/wiki/Opium "Opium"), a dark brown resin produced by drying the latex of [opium](/wiki/Opium "Opium") poppies (*[Papaver somniferum](/wiki/Papaver_somniferum "Papaver somniferum")*). It is mainly used as an [analgesic](/wiki/Analgesic "Analgesic") (pain medication). There are multiple methods used to administer morphine: oral; [sublingual](/wiki/Sublingual_administration "Sublingual administration"); via [inhalation](/wiki/Inhalation "Inhalation"); [injection into a muscle](/wiki/Intramuscular "Intramuscular"), [injection under the skin](/wiki/Subcutaneous_injection "Subcutaneous injection"), or injection into the [spinal cord](/wiki/Spinal_cord "Spinal cord") area; [transdermal](/wiki/Transdermal "Transdermal"); [intravenously](/wiki/Intravenous_therapy "Intravenous therapy"); or via [rectal](/wiki/Rectal_administration "Rectal administration") [suppository](/wiki/Suppository "Suppository").[[14]](#cite_note-AHFS2015-14)[[17]](#cite_note-St2012-17) It acts directly on the [central nervous system](/wiki/Central_nervous_system "Central nervous system") (CNS) to induce [analgesia](/wiki/Analgesia "Analgesia") and alter perception and emotional response to pain. Physical and psychological dependence and tolerance may develop with repeated administration.[[14]](#cite_note-AHFS2015-14) It can be taken for both [acute pain](/wiki/Acute_pain "Acute pain") and [chronic pain](/wiki/Chronic_pain "Chronic pain") and is frequently used for pain from [myocardial infarction](/wiki/Myocardial_infarction "Myocardial infarction"), [kidney stones](/wiki/Kidney_stone "Kidney stone"), and during [labor](/wiki/Childbirth "Childbirth").[[14]](#cite_note-AHFS2015-14) Its maximum effect is reached after about 20 minutes when administered intravenously and 60 minutes when administered by mouth, while the duration of its effect is 3–7 hours.[[14]](#cite_note-AHFS2015-14)[[15]](#cite_note-Rockwood2009-15) [Long-acting formulations of morphine](/wiki/Extended-release_morphine "Extended-release morphine") are sold under the brand names **MS Contin** and **Kadian**, among others. Generic long-acting formulations are also available.[[14]](#cite_note-AHFS2015-14)

Common side effects of morphine include [drowsiness](/wiki/Drowsiness "Drowsiness"), [euphoria](/wiki/Euphoria "Euphoria"), [nausea](/wiki/Nausea "Nausea"), [dizziness](/wiki/Dizziness "Dizziness"), [sweating](/wiki/Sweating "Sweating"), and [constipation](/wiki/Constipation "Constipation").[[14]](#cite_note-AHFS2015-14) Potentially serious [side effects](/wiki/Side_effects "Side effects") of morphine include [decreased respiratory effort](/wiki/Hypoventilation "Hypoventilation"), [vomiting](/wiki/Vomiting "Vomiting"), and [low blood pressure](/wiki/Low_blood_pressure "Low blood pressure").[[14]](#cite_note-AHFS2015-14) Morphine is highly [addictive](/wiki/Addiction "Addiction") and prone to [abuse](/wiki/Substance_abuse "Substance abuse").[[14]](#cite_note-AHFS2015-14) If one's dose is reduced after long-term use, [opioid withdrawal](/wiki/Opioid_withdrawal "Opioid withdrawal") symptoms may occur.[[14]](#cite_note-AHFS2015-14) Caution is advised for the use of morphine during [pregnancy](/wiki/Pregnancy "Pregnancy") or [breastfeeding](/wiki/Breastfeeding "Breastfeeding"), as it may affect the health of the baby.[[14]](#cite_note-AHFS2015-14)[[3]](#cite_note-Drugs.com_pregnancy-3)

Morphine was first isolated in 1804 by German pharmacist [Friedrich Sertürner](/wiki/Friedrich_Sert%C3%BCrner "Friedrich Sertürner").[[18]](#cite_note-pmid18443637-18)[[19]](#cite_note-Court2009-19) This is believed to be the first isolation of a medicinal alkaloid from a plant.[[20]](#cite_note-Luch2009-20) [Merck](/wiki/Merck_Group "Merck Group") began marketing it commercially in 1827.[[19]](#cite_note-Court2009-19) Morphine was more widely used after the invention of the [hypodermic syringe](/wiki/Hypodermic_syringe "Hypodermic syringe") in 1853–1855.[[19]](#cite_note-Court2009-19)[[21]](#cite_note-Clay2013-21) Sertürner originally named the substance *morphium*, after the Greek god of dreams, [Morpheus](/wiki/Morpheus_(mythology) "Morpheus (mythology)"), as it has a tendency to cause sleep.[[21]](#cite_note-Clay2013-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)

The primary source of morphine is isolation from [poppy straw](/wiki/Poppy_straw "Poppy straw") of the opium poppy.[[23]](#cite_note-23) In 2013, approximately 523 tons of morphine were produced.[[24]](#cite_note-UN2015-24) Approximately 45 tons were used directly for pain, an increase of 400% over the last twenty years.[[24]](#cite_note-UN2015-24) Most use for this purpose was in the [developed world](/wiki/Developed_world "Developed world").[[24]](#cite_note-UN2015-24) About 70% of morphine is used to make other [opioids](/wiki/Opioids "Opioids") such as [hydromorphone](/wiki/Hydromorphone "Hydromorphone"), [oxymorphone](/wiki/Oxymorphone "Oxymorphone"), and [heroin](/wiki/Heroin "Heroin").[[24]](#cite_note-UN2015-24)[[25]](#cite_note-Trig2006-25)[[26]](#cite_note-Kar2006-26) It is a [Schedule II drug](/wiki/List_of_Schedule_II_drugs_(US) "List of Schedule II drugs (US)") in the United States,[[25]](#cite_note-Trig2006-25) [Class A](/wiki/Class_A_drug "Class A drug") in the United Kingdom,[[6]](#cite_note-Black's-6) and [Schedule I](/wiki/Controlled_Drugs_and_Substances_Act "Controlled Drugs and Substances Act") in Canada.[[27]](#cite_note-27) It is on the [World Health Organization's List of Essential Medicines](/wiki/WHO_Model_List_of_Essential_Medicines "WHO Model List of Essential Medicines").[[28]](#cite_note-WHO22nd-28) In 2023, it was the 156th most commonly prescribed medication in the United States, with more than 3 million prescriptions.[[29]](#cite_note-Top_300-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30) It is available as a [generic medication](/wiki/Generic_medication "Generic medication").[[31]](#cite_note-31)

## Medical uses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Morphine&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Medical uses")]

### Pain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Morphine&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Pain")]

Morphine is used primarily to treat both acute and [chronic](/wiki/Chronic_pain "Chronic pain") severe [pain](/wiki/Pain "Pain"). Its duration of [analgesia](/wiki/Analgesia "Analgesia") is about three to seven hours.[[14]](#cite_note-AHFS2015-14)[[15]](#cite_note-Rockwood2009-15) Side effects of nausea and constipation are rarely severe enough to warrant stopping treatment.

It is used for pain due to [myocardial infarction](/wiki/Myocardial_infarction "Myocardial infarction") and for labor pains.[[14]](#cite_note-AHFS2015-14) However, concerns exist that morphine may increase mortality in the event of [non ST elevation myocardial infarction](/wiki/Non_ST_elevation_myocardial_infarction "Non ST elevation myocardial infarction").[[32]](#cite_note-pmid15976786-32)

Morphine has also traditionally been used in the treatment of [acute pulmonary edema](/wiki/Acute_pulmonary_edema "Acute pulmonary edema").[[14]](#cite_note-AHFS2015-14) However, a 2006 review found little evidence to support this practice.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

A 2016 Cochrane review concluded that morphine is effective in relieving [cancer pain](/wiki/Cancer_pain "Cancer pain").[[34]](#cite_note-Wiffen_2016-34)

### Shortness of breath

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Morphine&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Shortness of breath")]

Morphine is beneficial in reducing the symptom of [shortness of breath](/wiki/Shortness_of_breath "Shortness of breath") due to both [cancer](/wiki/Cancer "Cancer") and non-cancer causes.[[35]](#cite_note-Pal2010-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36) In the setting of breathlessness at rest or on minimal exertion from conditions such as advanced cancer or end-stage cardiorespiratory diseases, regular, low-dose sustained-release morphine significantly reduces breathlessness safely, with its benefits maintained over time.[[37]](#cite_note-pmid22336677-37)[[38]](#cite_note-pmid20202949-38)

### Opioid use disorder

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Morphine&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Opioid use disorder")]

Morphine is used in a slow-release formulation for [opiate substitution therapy](/wiki/Opiate_substitution_therapy "Opiate substitution therapy") (OST) in Austria, Germany, Bulgaria, Slovenia, and Canada for persons with [opioid addiction](/wiki/Opioid_addiction "Opioid addiction") who cannot tolerate either [methadone](/wiki/Methadone "Methadone") or [buprenorphine](/wiki/Buprenorphine "Buprenorphine").[[39]](#cite_note-NEPOD_Report-39)

## Contraindications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Morphine&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Contraindications")]

Relative [contraindications](/wiki/Contraindication "Contraindication") to morphine include:

* [Respiratory depression](/wiki/Hypoventilation "Hypoventilation") when appropriate equipment is not available.[[14]](#cite_note-AHFS2015-14)
* Although it has previously been thought that morphine was contraindicated in [acute pancreatitis](/wiki/Acute_pancreatitis "Acute pancreatitis"), a review of the literature shows no evidence for this.[[40]](#cite_note-pmid11316181-40)

A localized reaction to intravenous morphine caused by histamine release in the veins

### Constipation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Morphine&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Constipation")]

Like [loperamide](/wiki/Loperamide "Loperamide") and other opioids, morphine acts on the [myenteric plexus](/wiki/Myenteric_plexus "Myenteric plexus") in the intestinal tract, reducing gut motility, and causing constipation. The gastrointestinal effects of morphine are mediated primarily by [μ-opioid receptors](/wiki/Mu_Opioid_receptor "Mu Opioid receptor") in the bowel. By inhibiting gastric emptying and reducing propulsive [peristalsis](/wiki/Peristalsis "Peristalsis") of the intestine, morphine decreases the rate of intestinal transit. Reduction in gut secretion and increased intestinal fluid absorption also contribute to the constipating effect. Opioids also may act on the gut indirectly through tonic gut spasms after inhibition of [nitric oxide](/wiki/Nitric_oxide "Nitric oxide") generation.[[42]](#cite_note-pmid15082884-42) This effect was shown in animals when a nitric oxide precursor, [L-arginine](/wiki/L-arginine "L-arginine"), reversed morphine-induced changes in gut motility.[[43]](#cite_note-Calignano,_Moncada_and_Di_Rosa-43)

### Hormone imbalance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Morphine&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Hormone imbalance")]

See also: [Opioid § Hormone imbalance](/wiki/Opioid#Hormone_imbalance "Opioid")

Clinical studies consistently conclude that morphine, like other opioids, often causes [hypogonadism](/wiki/Hypogonadism "Hypogonadism") and [hormone imbalances](/wiki/Hormone_imbalance "Hormone imbalance") in chronic users of both sexes. This side effect is [dose-dependent](/wiki/Dose%E2%80%93response_relationship "Dose–response relationship") and occurs in both therapeutic and recreational users. Morphine can interfere with menstruation by suppressing levels of [luteinizing hormone](/wiki/Luteinizing_hormone "Luteinizing hormone"). Multiple studies suggest the majority (perhaps as many as 90%) of chronic opioid users have opioid-induced hypogonadism. This effect may cause the increased likelihood of [osteoporosis](/wiki/Osteoporosis "Osteoporosis") and [bone fracture](/wiki/Bone_fracture "Bone fracture") observed in chronic morphine users. Studies suggest the effect is temporary. As of 2013[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Morphine&action=edit), the effect of low-dose or acute use of morphine on the endocrine system is unclear.[[44]](#cite_note-pmid23414717-44)[[45]](#cite_note-pmid19193821-45)

### Effects on human performance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Morphine&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Effects on human performance")]

Most reviews conclude that opioids produce minimal impairment of human performance on tests of sensory, motor, or attentional abilities. However, recent studies have been able to show some impairments caused by morphine, which is not surprising, given that morphine is a central nervous system [depressant](/wiki/Depressant "Depressant"). Morphine has resulted in impaired functioning on critical flicker frequency (a measure of overall CNS arousal) and impaired performance on the [Maddox wing](/wiki/Maddox_wing "Maddox wing") test (a measure of the deviation of the visual axes of the eyes). Few studies have investigated the effects of morphine on motor abilities; a high dose of morphine can impair finger tapping and the ability to maintain a low constant level of [isometric force](/wiki/Isometric_exercise "Isometric exercise") (i.e. fine motor control is impaired),[[46]](#cite_note-pmid1755931-46) though no studies have shown a correlation between morphine and gross motor abilities.

In terms of [cognitive](/wiki/Cognitive "Cognitive") abilities, one study has shown that morphine may negatively impact [anterograde](/wiki/Anterograde_amnesia "Anterograde amnesia") and [retrograde memory](/wiki/Retrograde_amnesia "Retrograde amnesia"),[[47]](#cite_note-47) but these effects are minimal and transient. Overall, it seems that acute doses of opioids in non-tolerant subjects produce minor effects in some sensory and motor abilities, and perhaps also in [attention](/wiki/Attention "Attention") and cognition. The effects of morphine will likely be more pronounced in opioid-naive subjects than in chronic opioid users.

In chronic opioid users, such as those on Chronic Opioid Analgesic Therapy (COAT) for managing severe, [chronic pain](/wiki/Chronic_pain "Chronic pain"), behavioural testing has shown normal functioning on perception, cognition, coordination, and behaviour in most cases. One 2000 study[[48]](#cite_note-48) analysed COAT patients to determine whether they were able to safely operate a motor vehicle. The findings from this study suggest that stable opioid use does not significantly impair abilities inherent in driving (this includes physical, cognitive, and perceptual skills). COAT patients showed rapid completion of tasks that require the speed of responding for successful performance (e.g., [Rey Complex Figure](/wiki/Rey_Complex_Figure "Rey Complex Figure") Test) but made more errors than controls. COAT patients showed no deficits in visual-spatial perception and organization (as shown in the [WAIS-R](/wiki/Wechsler_Adult_Intelligence_Scale "Wechsler Adult Intelligence Scale") Block Design Test) but did show impaired immediate and short-term visual memory (as shown on the Rey Complex Figure Test – Recall). These patients showed no impairments in higher-order cognitive abilities (i.e., planning). COAT patients appeared to have difficulty following instructions and showed a propensity toward impulsive behaviour, yet this did not reach statistical significance. It is important to note that this study reveals that COAT patients have no domain-specific deficits, which supports the notion that chronic opioid use has minor effects on [psychomotor](/wiki/Psychomotor_learning "Psychomotor learning"), [cognitive](/wiki/Cognitive "Cognitive"), or [neuropsychological](/wiki/Neuropsychological "Neuropsychological") functioning.

### Reinforcement disorders

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Morphine&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Reinforcement disorders")]

#### Addiction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Morphine&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Addiction")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Santiago_Rusinol_Before_the_Morphine.jpg/250px-Santiago_Rusinol_Before_the_Morphine.jpg)](/wiki/File:Santiago_Rusinol_Before_the_Morphine.jpg)

*Before the Morphine* by [Santiago Rusiñol](/wiki/Santiago_Rusi%C3%B1ol "Santiago Rusiñol")

Morphine is a highly [addictive](/wiki/Addictive "Addictive") substance. Multiple studies, including one by *The Lancet*, ranked morphine/heroin as the #1 most addictive substance, followed by [cocaine](/wiki/Cocaine "Cocaine") at #2, [nicotine](/wiki/Nicotine "Nicotine") #3, [barbiturates](/wiki/Barbiturates "Barbiturates") at #4, and [ethanol](/wiki/Ethanol "Ethanol") at #5. In controlled studies comparing the physiological and subjective effects of [heroin](/wiki/Heroin "Heroin") and morphine in individuals formerly addicted to opiates, subjects showed no preference for one drug over the other. Equipotent, injected doses had comparable action courses, with heroin crossing the [blood–brain barrier](/wiki/Blood%E2%80%93brain_barrier "Blood–brain barrier") slightly quicker. No difference in subjects' self-rated feelings of [euphoria](/wiki/Euphoria "Euphoria"), ambition, nervousness, relaxation, or drowsiness.[[49]](#cite_note-martin_and_fraser-49) Short-term addiction studies by the same researchers demonstrated that tolerance developed at a similar rate to both heroin and morphine. When compared to the opioids hydromorphone, [fentanyl](/wiki/Fentanyl "Fentanyl"), [oxycodone](/wiki/Oxycodone "Oxycodone"), and [pethidine](/wiki/Pethidine "Pethidine"), former addicts showed a strong preference for heroin and morphine, suggesting that heroin and morphine are particularly susceptible to abuse and addiction. Morphine and heroin also produced higher rates of euphoria and other positive subjective effects when compared to these other opioids.[[49]](#cite_note-martin_and_fraser-49) The choice of heroin and morphine over other opioids by former drug addicts may also be because heroin is an ester of morphine and morphine [prodrug](/wiki/Prodrug "Prodrug"), essentially meaning they are identical drugs *in vivo*. Heroin is converted to morphine before binding to the [opioid receptors](/wiki/Opioid_receptor "Opioid receptor") in the brain and spinal cord, where morphine causes subjective effects, which is what the addicted individuals are seeking.[[50]](#cite_note-NIDA-2013-50)

#### Tolerance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Morphine&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Tolerance")]

Several hypotheses are given about how tolerance develops, including opioid receptor [phosphorylation](/wiki/Phosphorylation "Phosphorylation") (which would change the receptor conformation), functional decoupling of receptors from [G-proteins](/wiki/G-proteins "G-proteins") (leading to receptor desensitization),[[51]](#cite_note-51) μ-opioid receptor internalization or receptor down-regulation (reducing the number of available receptors for morphine to act on), and upregulation of the [cAMP](/wiki/Cyclic_adenosine_monophosphate "Cyclic adenosine monophosphate") pathway (a counterregulatory mechanism to opioid effects) (For a review of these processes, see Koch and Hollt[[52]](#cite_note-52)).

#### Dependence and withdrawal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Morphine&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Dependence and withdrawal")]

See also: [Opioid use disorder](/wiki/Opioid_use_disorder "Opioid use disorder") and [Opioid withdrawal](/wiki/Opioid_withdrawal "Opioid withdrawal")

|  |  |
| --- | --- |

Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Morphine"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Morphine%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Morphine%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Morphine%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Morphine%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Morphine%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Morphine%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(November 2019)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Cessation of dosing with morphine creates the prototypical opioid withdrawal syndrome, which, unlike that of [barbiturates](/wiki/Barbiturates "Barbiturates"), [benzodiazepines](/wiki/Benzodiazepines "Benzodiazepines"), [alcohol](/wiki/Alcohol_(drug) "Alcohol (drug)"), or [sedative](/wiki/Sedative "Sedative")-hypnotics, is not fatal by itself in otherwise healthy people.

Acute morphine withdrawal, along with that of any other opioid, proceeds through a number of stages. Other opioids differ in the intensity and length of each, and weak opioids and mixed agonist-antagonists may have acute withdrawal syndromes that do not reach the highest level. As commonly cited[*[by whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*], they are:

* **Stage I**, 6 h to 14 h after last dose: Drug craving, anxiety, irritability, perspiration, and mild to moderate [dysphoria](/wiki/Dysphoria "Dysphoria")
* **Stage II**, 14 h to 18 h after last dose: [Yawning](/wiki/Yawn "Yawn"), heavy [perspiration](/wiki/Perspiration "Perspiration"), mild [depression](/wiki/Depression_(mood) "Depression (mood)"), [lacrimation](/wiki/Lacrimation "Lacrimation"), [crying](/wiki/Crying "Crying"), headaches, runny nose, dysphoria, also intensification of the above symptoms, "yen sleep" (a waking trance-like state)
* **Stage III**, 16 h to 24 h after last dose: Increase in all of the above, [dilated pupils](/wiki/Mydriasis "Mydriasis"), [piloerection](/wiki/Goose_bumps "Goose bumps") (goose bumps),[[53]](#cite_note-53) muscle twitches, [hot flashes](/wiki/Hot_flash "Hot flash"), cold flashes, aching bones and muscles, [loss of appetite](/wiki/Anorexia_(symptom) "Anorexia (symptom)"), and the beginning of intestinal cramping
* **Stage IV**, 24 h to 36 h after last dose: Increase in all of the above including severe cramping, [restless legs syndrome](/wiki/Restless_leg_syndrome "Restless leg syndrome"), loose stool, [insomnia](/wiki/Insomnia "Insomnia"), elevation of blood pressure, [fever](/wiki/Fever "Fever"), increase in frequency of breathing and tidal volume, [tachycardia](/wiki/Tachycardia "Tachycardia") (elevated pulse), [restlessness](/wiki/Psychomotor_agitation "Psychomotor agitation"), nausea
* **Stage V**, 36 h to 72 h after last dose: Increase in all of the above, fetal position, vomiting, free and frequent liquid diarrhea, weight loss of 2 kg to 5 kg per 24 h, increased [white cell count](/wiki/White_blood_cell "White blood cell"), and other blood changes
* **Stage VI**, after completion of above: Recovery of appetite and normal bowel function, beginning of transition to [post-acute withdrawal symptoms](/wiki/Post-acute-withdrawal_syndrome "Post-acute-withdrawal syndrome") that are mainly psychological, but may also include increased sensitivity to pain, [hypertension](/wiki/Hypertension "Hypertension"), [colitis](/wiki/Colitis "Colitis") or other gastrointestinal afflictions related to motility, and problems with weight control in either direction

In advanced stages of withdrawal, ultrasonographic evidence of pancreatitis has been demonstrated in some patients and is presumably attributed to spasm of the pancreatic [sphincter of Oddi](/wiki/Sphincter_of_Oddi "Sphincter of Oddi").[[54]](#cite_note-54)

The withdrawal symptoms associated with morphine addiction are usually experienced shortly before the time of the next scheduled dose, sometimes within as early as a few hours (usually 6 h to 12 h) after the last administration. Early symptoms include watery eyes, insomnia, diarrhea, runny nose, yawning, [dysphoria](/wiki/Dysphoria "Dysphoria"), sweating, and, in some cases, a strong drug craving. Severe headache, restlessness, [irritability](/wiki/Irritability "Irritability"), loss of appetite, body aches, severe abdominal pain, nausea and vomiting, tremors, and even stronger and more intense drug craving appear as the syndrome progresses. Severe depression and vomiting are common. During the acute withdrawal period, systolic and diastolic blood pressures increase, usually beyond premorphine levels, and heart rate increases,[[55]](#cite_note-Chan,_Irvine,_and_White-55) which have potential to cause a heart attack, blood clot, or stroke.

Chills or cold flashes with goose bumps alternating with flushing (hot flashes), kicking movements of the legs,[[50]](#cite_note-NIDA-2013-50) and excessive sweating are also characteristic symptoms.[[56]](#cite_note-56) Severe pains in the bones and muscles of the back and extremities occur, as do muscle spasms. At any point during this process, a suitable narcotic can be administered that will dramatically reverse the withdrawal symptoms. Major withdrawal symptoms peak between 48 h and 96 h after the last dose and subside after about 8 to 12 days. Sudden discontinuation of morphine by heavily [dependent](/wiki/Drug_dependence "Drug dependence") users who are in poor health is rarely fatal. Morphine withdrawal is considered less dangerous than alcohol, barbiturate, or benzodiazepine withdrawal.[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58)

The psychological dependence associated with morphine [addiction](/wiki/Substance_use_disorder "Substance use disorder") is complex and protracted. Long after the physical need for morphine has passed, addicts will usually continue to think and talk about the use of morphine (or other drugs) and feel strange or overwhelmed coping with daily activities without being under the influence of morphine. Psychological withdrawal from morphine is usually a long and painful process. Addicts often experience severe depression, anxiety, insomnia, mood swings, forgetfulness, low [self-esteem](/wiki/Self-esteem "Self-esteem"), [confusion](/wiki/Confusion "Confusion"), [paranoia](/wiki/Paranoia "Paranoia"), and other psychological problems. Without intervention, the syndrome will run its course, and most of the overt physical symptoms will disappear within 7 to 10 days including psychological dependence. A high probability of relapse exists after morphine withdrawal when neither the physical environment nor the behavioral motivators that contributed to the abuse have been altered. Testimony of morphine's addictive and reinforcing nature is its relapse rate. Users of morphine have one of the highest relapse rates among all drug users, ranging up to 98% in the estimation of some medical experts.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

See also: [Opioid overdose](/wiki/Opioid_overdose "Opioid overdose")

| [Acidity](/wiki/Acidity "Acidity") (p*K*a)[[60]](#cite_note-CRC_85-60) | |  |  | | --- | --- | | Step 1: 8.21 | at 25 °C | | Step 2: 9.85 | at 20 °C | |

A large [overdose](/wiki/Overdose "Overdose") can cause [asphyxia](/wiki/Asphyxia "Asphyxia") and death by respiratory depression if the person does not receive medical attention immediately.[[61]](#cite_note-Duldner-61) Overdose treatment includes the administration of [naloxone](/wiki/Naloxone "Naloxone"). The latter completely reverses morphine's effects but may result in the immediate onset of withdrawal in opiate-addicted subjects. Multiple doses may be needed as the duration of action of morphine is longer than that of naloxone.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

## Pharmacology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Morphine&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Pharmacology")]

### Pharmacodynamics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Morphine&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Pharmacodynamics")]

Due to its long history and established use as a pain medication, this compound has become the benchmark to which all other opioids are compared.[[68]](#cite_note-68) It interacts predominantly with the μ–δ-opioid (Mu-Delta) [receptor heteromer](/wiki/GPCR_oligomer "GPCR oligomer").[[69]](#cite_note-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70) The μ-binding sites are discretely distributed in the [human brain](/wiki/Human_brain "Human brain"), with high densities in the posterior [amygdala](/wiki/Amygdala "Amygdala"), [hypothalamus](/wiki/Hypothalamus "Hypothalamus"), [thalamus](/wiki/Thalamus "Thalamus"), [nucleus caudatus](/wiki/Nucleus_caudatus "Nucleus caudatus"), [putamen](/wiki/Putamen "Putamen"), and certain cortical areas. They are also found on the [terminal axons](/wiki/Chemical_synapse "Chemical synapse") of primary afferents within laminae [I](/wiki/Posteromarginal_nucleus "Posteromarginal nucleus") and II ([substantia gelatinosa](/wiki/Substantia_gelatinosa_of_Rolando "Substantia gelatinosa of Rolando")) of the spinal cord and in the spinal nucleus of the [trigeminal nerve](/wiki/Trigeminal_nerve "Trigeminal nerve").[[71]](#cite_note-rxlist.com-71)

Morphine is a [phenanthrene](/wiki/Phenanthrene "Phenanthrene") [opioid receptor](/wiki/Opioid_receptor "Opioid receptor") [agonist](/wiki/Receptor_agonist "Receptor agonist") – its main effect is binding to and activating the [μ-opioid receptor](/wiki/%CE%9C-opioid_receptor "Μ-opioid receptor") (MOR) in the central nervous system. Its [intrinsic activity](/wiki/Intrinsic_activity "Intrinsic activity") at the MOR is heavily dependent on the [assay](/wiki/Assay "Assay") and tissue being tested; in some situations it is a [full agonist](/wiki/Full_agonist "Full agonist") while in others it can be a [partial agonist](/wiki/Partial_agonist "Partial agonist") or even [antagonist](/wiki/Receptor_antagonist "Receptor antagonist").[[72]](#cite_note-pmid23646826-72) In clinical settings, morphine exerts its principal pharmacological effect on the central nervous system and [gastrointestinal tract](/wiki/Human_gastrointestinal_tract "Human gastrointestinal tract"). Its primary actions of therapeutic value are analgesia and sedation. Activation of the MOR is associated with analgesia, sedation, [euphoria](/wiki/Euphoria_(emotion) "Euphoria (emotion)"), physical [dependence](/wiki/Chemical_dependency "Chemical dependency"), and [respiratory depression](/wiki/Respiratory_depression "Respiratory depression"). Morphine is also a [κ-opioid receptor](/wiki/%CE%9A-opioid_receptor "Κ-opioid receptor") (KOR) and [δ-opioid receptor](/wiki/%CE%94-opioid_receptor "Δ-opioid receptor") (DOR) agonist. Activation of the KOR is associated with spinal analgesia, [miosis](/wiki/Miosis "Miosis") (pinpoint pupils), and [psychotomimetic](/wiki/Psychotomimetic "Psychotomimetic") effects. The DOR is thought to play a role in analgesia.[[71]](#cite_note-rxlist.com-71)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] Although morphine does not bind to the [σ receptor](/wiki/Sigma_receptor "Sigma receptor"), it has been shown that σ receptor agonists, such as [(+)-pentazocine](/wiki/Pentazocine "Pentazocine"), inhibit morphine analgesia, and σ receptor antagonists enhance morphine analgesia,[[73]](#cite_note-73) suggesting downstream involvement of the σ receptor in the actions of morphine.

The effects of morphine can be countered with [opioid receptor antagonists](/wiki/Opioid_receptor_antagonist "Opioid receptor antagonist") such as [naloxone](/wiki/Naloxone "Naloxone") and [naltrexone](/wiki/Naltrexone "Naltrexone"); the development of tolerance to morphine may be inhibited by [NMDA receptor antagonists](/wiki/NMDA_receptor_antagonist "NMDA receptor antagonist") such as [ketamine](/wiki/Ketamine "Ketamine"), [dextromethorphan](/wiki/Dextromethorphan "Dextromethorphan"), and [memantine](/wiki/Memantine "Memantine").[[74]](#cite_note-pmid8747752-74)[[75]](#cite_note-75) The rotation of morphine with chemically dissimilar opioids in the long-term treatment of pain will slow down the growth of tolerance in the longer run, particularly agents known to have significantly incomplete cross-tolerance with morphine such as [levorphanol](/wiki/Levorphanol "Levorphanol"), [ketobemidone](/wiki/Ketobemidone "Ketobemidone"), [piritramide](/wiki/Piritramide "Piritramide"), and [methadone](/wiki/Methadone "Methadone") and its derivatives; all of these drugs also have NMDA antagonist properties. It is believed that the strong opioid with the most incomplete cross-tolerance with morphine is either methadone[[76]](#cite_note-76) or [dextromoramide](/wiki/Dextromoramide "Dextromoramide").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Morphine_Ampoule_For_Veterinary_Use.jpg/250px-Morphine_Ampoule_For_Veterinary_Use.jpg)](/wiki/File:Morphine_Ampoule_For_Veterinary_Use.jpg)

Morphine [hydrochloride](/wiki/Hydrochloride "Hydrochloride") ampoule for [veterinary use](/wiki/Veterinary_medicine "Veterinary medicine")

#### Analgesia creation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Morphine&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Analgesia creation")]

Morphine creates analgesia through the activation of a specific group of neurons in the [rostral ventromedial medulla](/wiki/Rostral_ventromedial_medulla "Rostral ventromedial medulla"), called the "morphine ensemble."[[77]](#cite_note-j866-77) This ensemble includes glutamatergic neurons that project to the spinal cord, known as RVMBDNF neurons. These neurons connect to inhibitory neurons in the spinal cord, called SCGal neurons, which release the neurotransmitter GABA and the neuropeptide [galanin](/wiki/Galanin "Galanin"). The inhibition of SCGal neurons is crucial for morphine's pain-relieving effects. Additionally, the neurotrophic factor [BDNF](/wiki/Brain-derived_neurotrophic_factor "Brain-derived neurotrophic factor"), produced within the RVMBDNF neurons, is required for morphine's action. Increasing BDNF levels enhances morphine's analgesic effects, even at lower doses.[[78]](#cite_note-v878-78)[[77]](#cite_note-j866-77)

#### Gene expression

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Morphine&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Gene expression")]

Studies have shown that morphine can alter the expression of several [genes](/wiki/Genes "Genes"). A single injection of morphine has been shown to alter the expression of two major groups of genes, for proteins involved in [mitochondrial](/wiki/Mitochondria "Mitochondria") respiration and for [cytoskeleton](/wiki/Cytoskeleton "Cytoskeleton")-related proteins.[[79]](#cite_note-79)

#### Effects on the immune system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Morphine&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Effects on the immune system")]

Morphine has long been known to act on receptors expressed in cells of the central nervous system resulting in pain relief and analgesia. In the 1970s and '80s, evidence suggesting that people addicted to opioids show an increased risk of infection (such as increased [pneumonia](/wiki/Pneumonia "Pneumonia"), [tuberculosis](/wiki/Tuberculosis "Tuberculosis"), and [HIV/AIDS](/wiki/HIV/AIDS "HIV/AIDS")) led scientists to believe that morphine may also affect the [immune system](/wiki/Immune_system "Immune system"). This possibility increased interest in the effect of chronic morphine use on the immune system.[[80]](#cite_note-80)

The first step in determining that morphine may affect the immune system was to establish that the opiate receptors known to be expressed on cells of the central nervous system are also expressed on cells of the immune system. One study successfully showed that [dendritic cells](/wiki/Dendritic_cells "Dendritic cells"), part of the innate immune system, display opiate receptors. Dendritic cells are responsible for producing [cytokines](/wiki/Cytokine "Cytokine"), which are the tools for communication in the immune system. This same study showed that dendritic cells chronically treated with morphine during their differentiation produce more [interleukin-12](/wiki/Interleukin-12 "Interleukin-12") (IL-12), a cytokine responsible for promoting the proliferation, growth, and differentiation of T-cells (another cell of the adaptive immune system) and less [interleukin-10](/wiki/Interleukin-10 "Interleukin-10") (IL-10), a cytokine responsible for promoting a B-cell immune response (B cells produce antibodies to fight off infection).[[81]](#cite_note-messmer_2006-81)

This regulation of cytokines appears to occur via the [p38 MAPKs (mitogen-activated protein kinase)-dependent pathway](/wiki/P38_MAPK_pathway "P38 MAPK pathway"). Usually, the p38 within the dendritic cell expresses [TLR 4](/wiki/TLR_4 "TLR 4") (toll-like receptor 4), which is activated through the ligand LPS ([lipopolysaccharide](/wiki/Lipopolysaccharide "Lipopolysaccharide")). This causes the p38 MAPK to be [phosphorylated](/wiki/Phosphorylation "Phosphorylation"). This phosphorylation activates the [p38 MAPK](/wiki/P38_mitogen-activated_protein_kinases "P38 mitogen-activated protein kinases") to begin producing IL-10 and IL-12. When the dendritic cells are chronically exposed to morphine during their differentiation process and then treated with LPS, the production of cytokines is different. Once treated with morphine, the p38 MAPK does not produce IL-10, instead favoring the production of IL-12. The exact mechanism through which the production of one cytokine is increased in favor over another is not known. Most likely, the morphine causes increased phosphorylation of the p38 MAPK. Transcriptional level interactions between IL-10 and IL-12 may further increase the production of IL-12 once IL-10 is not being produced. This increased production of IL-12 causes increased T-cell immune response.

Further studies on the effects of morphine on the immune system have shown that morphine influences the production of [neutrophils](/wiki/Neutrophils "Neutrophils") and other [cytokines](/wiki/Cytokines "Cytokines"). Since cytokines are produced as part of the immediate immunological response ([inflammation](/wiki/Inflammation "Inflammation")), it has been suggested that they may also influence pain. In this way, cytokines may be a logical target for analgesic development. Recently, one study has used an animal model (hind-paw incision) to observe the effects of morphine administration on the acute immunological response. Following the hind-paw incision, pain thresholds and cytokine production were measured. Normally, cytokine production in and around the wounded area increases to fight [infection](/wiki/Infection "Infection") and control healing (and, possibly, to control pain), but pre-incisional morphine administration (0.1 mg/kg to 10.0 mg/kg) reduced the number of cytokines found around the wound in a dose-dependent manner. The authors suggest that morphine administration in the acute post-injury period may reduce resistance to infection and may impair the healing of the wound.[[82]](#cite_note-pmid17908329-82)

### Pharmacokinetics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Morphine&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Pharmacokinetics")]

#### Absorption and metabolism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Morphine&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Absorption and metabolism")]

Morphine can be taken [orally](/wiki/Oral_administration "Oral administration"), sublingually, [bucally](/wiki/Buccal_space "Buccal space"), rectally, subcutaneously, [intranasally](/wiki/Insufflation_(medicine) "Insufflation (medicine)"), [intravenously](/wiki/Intravenous "Intravenous"), [intrathecally](/wiki/Intrathecal "Intrathecal") or [epidurally](/wiki/Epidural "Epidural") and inhaled via a nebulizer. As a recreational drug, it is becoming more common to inhale ("[Chasing the Dragon](/wiki/Chasing_the_Dragon "Chasing the Dragon")"), but, for medical purposes, intravenous (IV) injection is the most common method of administration. Morphine is subject to extensive [first-pass metabolism](/wiki/First-pass_metabolism "First-pass metabolism") (a large proportion is broken down in the liver), so, if taken orally, only 40% to 50% of the dose reaches the central nervous system. Resultant plasma levels after subcutaneous (SC), intramuscular (IM), and IV injection are all comparable. After IM or SC injections, morphine plasma levels peak in approximately 20 min, and, after oral administration, levels peak in approximately 30 min.[[83]](#cite_note-83) Morphine is [metabolised](/wiki/Metabolised "Metabolised") primarily in the [liver](/wiki/Liver "Liver") and approximately 87% of a dose of morphine is excreted in the [urine](/wiki/Urine "Urine") within 72 h of administration. Morphine is metabolized primarily into [morphine-3-glucuronide](/wiki/Morphine-3-glucuronide "Morphine-3-glucuronide") (M3G) and [morphine-6-glucuronide](/wiki/Morphine-6-glucuronide "Morphine-6-glucuronide") (M6G)[[84]](#cite_note-Kilpatrick_2005-84) via [glucuronidation](/wiki/Glucuronidation "Glucuronidation") by phase II metabolism enzyme [UDP-glucuronosyl transferase-2B7](/wiki/UGT2B7 "UGT2B7") (UGT2B7). About 60% of morphine is converted to M3G, and 6% to 10% is converted to M6G.[[85]](#cite_note-van_Dorp-85) Not only does the metabolism occur in the liver but it may also take place in the brain and the kidneys. M3G does not undergo opioid receptor binding and has no analgesic effect. M6G binds to μ-receptors and is half as potent an analgesic as morphine in humans.[[85]](#cite_note-van_Dorp-85) Morphine may also be metabolized into small amounts of [normorphine](/wiki/Normorphine "Normorphine"), codeine, and hydromorphone. Metabolism rate is determined by gender, age, diet, genetic makeup, disease state (if any), and use of other medications. The elimination [half-life](/wiki/Half-life "Half-life") of morphine is approximately 120 min, though there may be slight differences between men and women. Morphine can be stored in fat, and, thus, can be detectable even after death. Morphine can cross the [blood–brain barrier](/wiki/Blood%E2%80%93brain_barrier "Blood–brain barrier"), but, because of poor lipid solubility, protein binding, rapid conjugation with glucuronic acid, and ionization, it does not cross easily. [Heroin](/wiki/Heroin "Heroin"), which is derived from morphine, crosses the blood-brain barrier more easily, making it more potent.[[86]](#cite_note-Jenkins_AJ_2008-86)

#### Extended-release

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Morphine&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Extended-release")]

Main article: [Extended-release morphine](/wiki/Extended-release_morphine "Extended-release morphine")

There are [extended-release](/wiki/Time_release_technology "Time release technology") formulations of orally administered morphine whose effect lasts longer, which can be given once per day. Brand names for this formulation of morphine include Avinza,[[87]](#cite_note-maryland-87) Kadian,[[87]](#cite_note-maryland-87) MS Contin,[[87]](#cite_note-maryland-87) Dolcontin, and DepoDur.[[88]](#cite_note-PedersenFredheim2015-88) For constant pain, the relieving effect of extended-release morphine given once (for Kadian)[[89]](#cite_note-medscape-89) or twice (for MS Contin)[[89]](#cite_note-medscape-89) every 24 hours is roughly the same as multiple administrations of *immediate release* (or "regular") morphine.[[90]](#cite_note-northwestern-90) Extended-release morphine can be administered together with "rescue doses" of immediate-release morphine as needed in case of breakthrough pain, each generally consisting of 5% to 15% of the 24-hour extended-release dosage.[[90]](#cite_note-northwestern-90)

#### Detection in body fluids

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Morphine&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Detection in body fluids")]

Morphine and its major metabolites, morphine-3-glucuronide, and morphine-6-glucuronide, can be detected in blood, plasma, hair, and urine using an [immunoassay](/wiki/Immunoassay "Immunoassay"). [Chromatography](/wiki/Chromatography "Chromatography") can be used to test for each of these substances individually. Some testing procedures [hydrolyze](/wiki/Hydrolysis "Hydrolysis") metabolic products into morphine before the immunoassay, which must be considered when comparing morphine levels in separately published results. Morphine can also be isolated from whole blood samples by [solid phase extraction](/wiki/Solid_phase_extraction "Solid phase extraction") (SPE) and detected using [liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry](/wiki/Liquid_chromatography-mass_spectrometry "Liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry") (LC-MS).

Ingestion of codeine or food containing poppy seeds can cause false positives.[[91]](#cite_note-91)

A 1999 review estimated that relatively low doses of heroin (which metabolizes immediately into morphine) are detectable by standard urine tests for 1–1.5 days after use.[[92]](#cite_note-pmid11484423-92) A 2009 review determined that, when the [analyte](/wiki/Analyte "Analyte") is morphine and the [limit of detection](/wiki/Limit_of_detection "Limit of detection") is 1 ng/ml, a 20 mg intravenous (IV) dose of morphine is detectable for 12–24 hours. A limit of detection of 0.6 ng/ml had similar results.[[93]](#cite_note-pmid15228165-93)

### Chirality and biological activity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Morphine&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Chirality and biological activity")]

Morphine is a pentacyclic 3°amine (alkaloid) with **5 stereogenic centers** and exists in **32 stereoisomeric forms**. But the desired analgesic activity resides exclusively in the natural product, the (-)-enantiomer with the configuration (**5R,6S,9R,13S,14R).**[[94]](#cite_note-94)[[95]](#cite_note-95)

## Natural occurrence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Morphine&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Natural occurrence")]

See also: [Opium](/wiki/Opium "Opium")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Slaapbol_R0017600.JPG/250px-Slaapbol_R0017600.JPG)](/wiki/File:Slaapbol_R0017600.JPG)

Latex bleeding from a freshly-scored seed pod

Morphine is the most abundant opiate found in opium, the dried [latex](/wiki/Latex "Latex") extracted by shallowly scoring the unripe seedpods of the *[Papaver somniferum](/wiki/Papaver_somniferum "Papaver somniferum")* poppy. Morphine is generally 8–14% of the dry weight of opium.[[96]](#cite_note-Kapoor-96) Przemko and Norman cultivars of the opium poppy, are used to produce two other alkaloids, [thebaine](/wiki/Thebaine "Thebaine") and [oripavine](/wiki/Oripavine "Oripavine"), which are used in the manufacture of semi-synthetic and synthetic opioids like [oxycodone](/wiki/Oxycodone "Oxycodone") and [etorphine](/wiki/Etorphine "Etorphine"). *[P. bracteatum](/wiki/Papaver_bracteatum "Papaver bracteatum")* does not contain morphine or codeine, or other narcotic [phenanthrene](/wiki/Phenanthrene "Phenanthrene")-type, alkaloids. This species is rather a source of thebaine.[[97]](#cite_note-pmid925935-97) Occurrence of morphine in other [Papaverales](/wiki/Papaverales "Papaverales") and [Papaveraceae](/wiki/Papaveraceae "Papaveraceae"), as well as in some species of [hops](/wiki/Hops "Hops") and [mulberry](/wiki/Mulberry "Mulberry") trees has not been confirmed. Morphine is produced most predominantly early in the life cycle of the plant. Past the optimum point for [extraction](/wiki/Extraction_(chemistry) "Extraction (chemistry)") various processes in the plant produce codeine, thebaine, and in some cases negligible amounts of hydromorphone, [dihydromorphine](/wiki/Dihydromorphine "Dihydromorphine"), [dihydrocodeine](/wiki/Dihydrocodeine "Dihydrocodeine"), tetrahydro-thebaine, and hydrocodone (these compounds are rather synthesized from thebaine and oripavine).

In the brains of mammals, morphine is detectable in trace steady-state concentrations.[[17]](#cite_note-St2012-17) The human body also produces [endorphins](/wiki/Endorphins "Endorphins"), which are chemically related [endogenous opioid](/wiki/Endogenous_opioid "Endogenous opioid") [peptides](/wiki/Peptide "Peptide") that function as [neuropeptides](/wiki/Neuropeptide "Neuropeptide") and have similar effects to morphine.[[98]](#cite_note-98)

### Human biosynthesis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Morphine&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Human biosynthesis")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion** with: a more standard presentation, without a scheme-in-text, and with a description of key enzymes, points of pathway regulation, etc. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Morphine&action=edit&section=). *(October 2016)* |

Morphine is an [endogenous opioid](/wiki/Endogenous_opioid "Endogenous opioid") in humans. Various human cells are capable of synthesizing and releasing it, including [white blood cells](/wiki/White_blood_cell "White blood cell").[[17]](#cite_note-St2012-17)[[99]](#cite_note-IUPHAR_-_μ-opioid_receptor-99)[[100]](#cite_note-Poeaknapo_2004-100) The primary biosynthetic pathway for morphine in humans consists of[[17]](#cite_note-St2012-17)

[![Morphine biosynthesis in humans](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Morphine_biosynthesis_in_humans.png/960px-Morphine_biosynthesis_in_humans.png)](/wiki/File:Morphine_biosynthesis_in_humans.png)

Morphine biosynthesis in humans

:   [L-tyrosine](/wiki/L-tyrosine "L-tyrosine") → [*para*-tyramine](/wiki/Tyramine "Tyramine") or [L-DOPA](/wiki/L-DOPA "L-DOPA") → [Dopamine](/wiki/Dopamine "Dopamine")
:   L-tyrosine → L-DOPA → [3,4-dihydroxyphenylacetaldehyde](/wiki/3,4-dihydroxyphenylacetaldehyde "3,4-dihydroxyphenylacetaldehyde") (DOPAL)
:   Dopamine + DOPAL → [(*S*)-norlaudanosoline](/wiki/Tetrahydropapaveroline "Tetrahydropapaveroline") →→→ (*S*)-[reticuline](/wiki/Reticuline "Reticuline") → [1,2-dehydroreticulinium](/w/index.php?title=1,2-dehydroreticulinium&action=edit&redlink=1 "1,2-dehydroreticulinium (page does not exist)") → (*R*)-reticuline → [salutaridine](/wiki/Salutaridine "Salutaridine") → [salutaridinol](/wiki/Salutaridinol "Salutaridinol") → thebaine → [neopinone](/w/index.php?title=Neopinone&action=edit&redlink=1 "Neopinone (page does not exist)") → [codeinone](/wiki/Codeinone "Codeinone") → codeine → morphine

The intermediate (*S*)-norlaudanosoline (also known as tetrahydropapaveroline) is synthesized through the addition of DOPAL and dopamine.[[17]](#cite_note-St2012-17) [CYP2D6](/wiki/CYP2D6 "CYP2D6"), a [cytochrome P450](/wiki/Cytochrome_P450 "Cytochrome P450") isoenzyme is involved in two steps along the biosynthetic pathway, catalyzing both the biosynthesis of dopamine from tyramine and of morphine from codeine.[[17]](#cite_note-St2012-17)[[101]](#cite_note-CYP2D6_tyramine-dopamine_metabolism-101)

Urinary concentrations of endogenous codeine and morphine have been found to significantly increase in individuals taking [L-DOPA](/wiki/L-DOPA "L-DOPA") for the treatment of [Parkinson's disease](/wiki/Parkinson%27s_disease "Parkinson's disease").[[17]](#cite_note-St2012-17)

### Biosynthesis in the opium poppy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Morphine&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Biosynthesis in the opium poppy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Morphine_biosynthesis.png/960px-Morphine_biosynthesis.png)](/wiki/File:Morphine_biosynthesis.png)

Morphine biosynthesis in the opium poppy

Biosynthesis of morphine in the opium poppy begins with two tyrosine derivatives, dopamine and [4-hydroxyphenylacetaldehyde](/wiki/4-hydroxyphenylacetaldehyde "4-hydroxyphenylacetaldehyde"). Condensation of these precursors yields the primary intermediate [higenamine](/wiki/Higenamine "Higenamine") (norcoclaurine).[[102]](#cite_note-102) Subsequent action of four enzymes yields the tetrahydroisoquinoline reticuline, which is converted into salutaridine, thebaine, and oripavine. The enzymes involved in this process are the [salutaridine synthase](/wiki/Salutaridine_synthase "Salutaridine synthase"), [salutaridine:NADPH 7-oxidoreductase](/wiki/Salutaridine_reductase_(NADPH) "Salutaridine reductase (NADPH)") and the [codeinone reductase](/wiki/Codeinone_reductase_(NADPH) "Codeinone reductase (NADPH)").[[103]](#cite_note-103) Researchers are attempting to reproduce the biosynthetic pathway that produces morphine in [genetically engineered](/wiki/Genetically_engineered "Genetically engineered") [yeast](/wiki/Yeast "Yeast").[[104]](#cite_note-104) In June 2015 the *S*-reticuline could be produced from sugar and *R*-reticuline could be converted to morphine, but the intermediate reaction could not be performed.[[105]](#cite_note-105) In August 2015 the first complete synthesis of thebaine and hydrocodone in yeast was reported, but the process would need to be 100,000 times more productive to be suitable for commercial use.[[106]](#cite_note-106)[[107]](#cite_note-107)

Elements of the morphine structure have been used to create completely synthetic drugs such as the [morphinan](/wiki/Morphinan "Morphinan") family ([levorphanol](/wiki/Levorphanol "Levorphanol"), [dextromethorphan](/wiki/Dextromethorphan "Dextromethorphan") and others) and other groups that have multiple members with morphine-like qualities.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The modification of morphine and the aforementioned synthetics has also given rise to non-narcotic drugs with other uses such as emetics, stimulants, antitussives, anticholinergics, muscle relaxants, local anaesthetics, general anaesthetics, and others.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Morphine-derived [agonist–antagonist](/wiki/Agonist%E2%80%93antagonist "Agonist–antagonist") drugs have also been developed.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Structure description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Morphine&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Structure description")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Benzylisoquinoline_structure_in_Morphine.svg/250px-Benzylisoquinoline_structure_in_Morphine.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Benzylisoquinoline_structure_in_Morphine.svg)

Chemical structure of morphine. The [benzylisoquinoline](/wiki/Benzylisoquinoline "Benzylisoquinoline") backbone is shown in green.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Morphine_numbered.svg/250px-Morphine_numbered.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Morphine_numbered.svg)

Morphine structure showing its standard ring lettering and carbon numbering system[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Morphine_chemical_structure_in_3D.png/250px-Morphine_chemical_structure_in_3D.png)](/wiki/File:Morphine_chemical_structure_in_3D.png)

Same structure, but in a three-dimensional perspective

Morphine is a [benzylisoquinoline](/wiki/Benzylisoquinoline "Benzylisoquinoline") alkaloid with two additional ring closures.[[108]](#cite_note-108) As Jack DeRuiter of the Department of Drug Discovery and Development (formerly, Pharmacal Sciences), Harrison School of Pharmacy, Auburn University stated in his Fall 2000 course notes for that earlier department's "Principles of Drug Action 2" course, "Examination of the morphine molecule reveals the following structural features important to its pharmacological profile...

> 1. A rigid [pentacyclic](/wiki/Pentacyclic "Pentacyclic") structure consisting of a [benzene](/wiki/Benzene "Benzene") ring (A), two partially unsaturated [cyclohexane](/wiki/Cyclohexane "Cyclohexane") rings (B and C), a [piperidine](/wiki/Piperidine "Piperidine") ring (D) and a [tetrahydrofuran](/wiki/Tetrahydrofuran "Tetrahydrofuran") ring (E). Rings A, B, and C are the [phenanthrene](/wiki/Phenanthrene "Phenanthrene") ring system. This ring system has little conformational flexibility...
> 2. Two hydroxyl functional groups: a C3-[phenolic](/wiki/Phenol "Phenol") [hydroxyl group] (p*K*a 9.9) and a C6-[allylic](/wiki/Allyl "Allyl") [hydroxyl group],
> 3. An [ether](/wiki/Ether "Ether") linkage between E4 and E5,
> 4. [Unsaturation](/wiki/Saturated_and_unsaturated_compounds "Saturated and unsaturated compounds") between C7 and C8,
> 5. A basic, [tertiary]-amine function at position 17, [and]
> 6. [Five] centers of chirality (C5, C6, C9, C13, and C14) with morphine exhibiting a high degree of stereoselectivity of analgesic action."[[109]](#cite_note-urlwww.auburn.edu-109)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*][*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

Morphine and most of its derivatives do not exhibit optical isomerism, although some more distant relatives like the morphinan series ([levorphanol](/wiki/Levorphanol "Levorphanol"), [dextrorphan](/wiki/Dextrorphan "Dextrorphan"), and the racemic parent chemical [racemorphan](/wiki/Racemorphan "Racemorphan")) do,[[110]](#cite_note-110) and as noted above stereoselectivity in vivo is an important issue.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Uses and derivatives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Morphine&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Uses and derivatives")]

Most of the licit morphine produced is used to make codeine by methylation.[[111]](#cite_note-111) It is also a precursor for a number of drugs including [heroin](/wiki/Heroin "Heroin") (3,6-diacetylmorphine), hydromorphone (dihydromorphinone), and oxymorphone (14-hydroxydihydromorphinone).[[112]](#cite_note-112) Most semi-synthetic opioids, both of the morphine and codeine subgroups, are created by modifying one or more of the following:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* Halogenating or making other modifications at positions 1 or 2 on the morphine carbon skeleton.
* The methyl group that makes morphine into codeine can be removed or added back, or replaced with another functional group like ethyl and others to make codeine analogues of morphine-derived drugs and vice versa. codeine analogues of morphine-based drugs often serve as prodrugs of the stronger drug, as in codeine and morphine, hydrocodone and hydromorphone, oxycodone and oxymorphone, nicocodeine and [nicomorphine](/wiki/Nicomorphine "Nicomorphine"), dihydrocodeine and dihydromorphine, etc.
* Saturating, opening, or other changes to the bond between positions 7 and 8, as well as adding, removing, or modifying functional groups to these positions; saturating, reducing, eliminating, or otherwise modifying the 7–8 bond and attaching a functional group at 14 yields [hydromorphinol](/wiki/Hydromorphinol "Hydromorphinol"); the oxidation of the hydroxyl group to a carbonyl and changing the 7–8 bond to single from double changes codeine into oxycodone.
* Attachment, removal, or modification of functional groups to positions 3 or 6 (dihydrocodeine and related, hydrocodone, nicomorphine); in the case of moving the methyl functional group from position 3 to 6, codeine becomes [heterocodeine](/wiki/Heterocodeine "Heterocodeine"), which is 72 times stronger, and therefore six times stronger than morphine
* Attachment of functional groups or other modification at position 14 (oxymorphone, oxycodone, naloxone)
* Modifications at positions 2, 4, 5, or 17, usually along with other changes to the molecule elsewhere on the morphine skeleton. Often this is done with drugs produced by catalytic reduction, hydrogenation, oxidation, or the like, producing strong derivatives of morphine and codeine.

Many morphine derivatives can also be manufactured using thebaine or codeine as a starting material.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Replacement of the *N*-methyl group of morphine with an *N*-phenylethyl group results in a product that is 18 times more powerful than morphine in its opiate agonist potency.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Combining this modification with the replacement of the 6-[hydroxyl](/wiki/Hydroxyl "Hydroxyl") with a 6-[methylene group](/wiki/Methylene_group "Methylene group") produces a compound some 1,443 times more potent than morphine, stronger than the [Bentley compounds](/wiki/Bentley_compounds "Bentley compounds") such as [etorphine](/wiki/Etorphine "Etorphine") (M99, the Immobilon tranquilliser dart) by some measures.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Closely related to morphine are the opioids morphine-*N*-oxide (genomorphine), which is a pharmaceutical that is no longer in common use;[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and pseudomorphine, an alkaloid that exists in opium, form as degradation products of morphine.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

As a result of the extensive study and use of this molecule, more than 250 morphine derivatives (also counting codeine and related drugs) have been developed since the last quarter of the 19th century.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] These drugs range from 25% the analgesic strength of codeine (or slightly more than 2% of the strength of morphine) to several thousand times the strength of morphine, to powerful opioid antagonists, including [naloxone](/wiki/Naloxone "Naloxone") (Narcan), [naltrexone](/wiki/Naltrexone "Naltrexone") (Trexan), [diprenorphine](/wiki/Diprenorphine "Diprenorphine") (M5050, the reversing agent for the Immobilon dart) and [nalorphine](/wiki/Nalorphine "Nalorphine") (Nalline).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Some opioid agonist-antagonists, partial agonists, and inverse agonists are also derived from morphine.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The receptor-activation profile of the semi-synthetic morphine derivatives varies widely and some, like [apomorphine](/wiki/Apomorphine "Apomorphine") are devoid of narcotic effects.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Chemical salts of Morphine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Morphine&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Chemical salts of Morphine")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |

Relevant discussion may be found on the [talk page](/wiki/Talk:Morphine#Need_for_expert_attention "Talk:Morphine"). Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Morphine "Special:EditPage/Morphine") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this section. Both morphine and its hydrated form are sparingly soluble in water.[[113]](#cite_note-113) For this reason, pharmaceutical companies produce sulfate and [hydrochloride salts](/wiki/Hydrochloride "Hydrochloride") of the drug, both of which are over 300 times more water-soluble than their parent molecule.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*][*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Whereas the pH of a saturated morphine hydrate solution is 8.5, the salts are acidic.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Since they derive from a strong acid but weak base, they are both at about pH = 5;[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*][*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] as a consequence, the morphine salts are mixed with small amounts of [NaOH](/wiki/Sodium_hydroxide "Sodium hydroxide") to make them suitable for injection.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Many salts of morphine are used, with the most common in current clinical use being the hydrochloride, sulfate, tartrate, and citrate;[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] less commonly methobromide, hydrobromide, hydroiodide, lactate, chloride, and bitartrate and the others listed below.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Morphine diacetate (heroin) is not a salt, but rather a further derivative,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] see above.[[114]](#cite_note-114)

Morphine meconate is a major form of the alkaloid in the poppy, as is morphine pectinate, nitrate, sulfate, and some others.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Like codeine, dihydrocodeine and other (especially older) opiates, morphine has been used as the salicylate salt by some suppliers and can be easily compounded, imparting the therapeutic advantage of both the opioid and the [NSAID](/wiki/NSAID "NSAID");[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] multiple [barbiturate](/wiki/Barbiturate "Barbiturate") salts of morphine were also used in the past, as was/is morphine valerate, the salt of the acid being the active principle of [valerian](/wiki/Valerian_(herb) "Valerian (herb)").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Calcium morphenate](/wiki/Calcium_morphenate "Calcium morphenate") is the intermediate in various latex and poppy-straw methods of morphine production, more rarely sodium morphenate takes its place.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Morphine ascorbate and other salts such as the tannate, citrate, and acetate, phosphate, valerate and others may be present in poppy tea depending on the method of preparation.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][[115]](#cite_note-115)

The salts listed by the [United States Drug Enforcement Administration](/wiki/United_States_Drug_Enforcement_Administration "United States Drug Enforcement Administration") for reporting purposes, in addition to a few others, are as follows:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Morphine&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Production")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Alkaloids.png/330px-Alkaloids.png)](/wiki/File:Alkaloids.png)

First generation production of alkaloids from licit latex-derived opium

In the opium poppy, the alkaloids are bound to [meconic acid](/wiki/Meconic_acid "Meconic acid"). The method is to extract from the crushed plant with diluted sulfuric acid, which is a stronger acid than meconic acid, but not so strong to react with alkaloid molecules. The extraction is performed in multiple steps (one amount of crushed plant is extracted at least six to ten times, so practically every [alkaloid](/wiki/Alkaloid "Alkaloid") goes into the solution). From the solution obtained at the last extraction step, the alkaloids are precipitated by either ammonium hydroxide or sodium carbonate. The last step is purifying and separating morphine from other opium alkaloids. The somewhat similar Gregory process was developed in the United Kingdom during the Second World War, which begins with stewing the entire plant, in most cases save the roots and leaves, in plain or mildly acidified water, then proceeding through steps of concentration, extraction, and purification of alkaloids.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Other methods of processing "poppy straw" (i.e., dried pods and stalks) use steam, one or more of several types of alcohol, or other organic solvents.

The poppy straw methods predominate in Continental Europe and the British Commonwealth, with the latex method in most common use in India. The latex method can involve either vertical or horizontal slicing of the unripe pods with a two-to five-bladed knife with a guard developed specifically for this purpose to the depth of a fraction of a millimetre and scoring of the pods can be done up to five times. An alternative latex method sometimes used in China in the past is to cut off the poppy heads, run a large needle through them, and collect the dried latex 24 to 48 hours later.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In India, opium harvested by licensed poppy farmers is dehydrated to uniform levels of hydration at government processing centers and then sold to pharmaceutical companies that extract morphine from the opium. However, in Turkey and Tasmania, morphine is obtained by harvesting and processing the fully mature dry seed pods with attached stalks, called *poppy straw*. In Turkey, a water extraction process is used, while in Tasmania, a solvent extraction process is used.[[117]](#cite_note-117)

Opium poppy contains at least 50 different alkaloids, but most of them are of low concentration. Morphine is the principal alkaloid in raw opium and constitutes roughly 8–19% of opium by dry weight (depending on growing conditions).[[86]](#cite_note-Jenkins_AJ_2008-86) Some purpose-developed strains of poppy now produce opium that is up to 26% morphine by weight.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] A rough rule of thumb to determine the morphine content of pulverised dried poppy straw is to divide the percentage expected for the strain or crop via the latex method by eight or an empirically determined factor, which is often in the range of 5 to 15.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The Norman strain of *P. somniferum*, also developed in [Tasmania](/wiki/Tasmania "Tasmania"), produces down to 0.04% morphine but with much higher amounts of thebaine and oripavine, which can be used to synthesise semi-synthetic opioids as well as other drugs like stimulants, emetics, opioid antagonists, anticholinergics, and smooth-muscle agents.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the 1950s and 1960s, [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary") supplied nearly 60% of Europe's total medication-purpose morphine production. To this day, poppy farming is legal in Hungary, but poppy farms are limited by law to 2 acres (8,100 m2). It is also legal to sell dried poppies in flower shops for use in floral arrangements.

It was announced in 1973 that a team at the National Institutes of Health in the United States had developed a method for total synthesis of morphine, codeine, and thebaine using coal tar as a starting material. A shortage in codeine-hydrocodone class cough suppressants (all of which can be made from morphine in one or more steps, as well as from codeine or thebaine) was the initial reason for the research.

Most morphine produced for pharmaceutical use around the world is converted into codeine as the concentration of the latter in both raw opium and poppy straw is much lower than that of morphine; in most countries, the usage of codeine (both as end-product and precursor) is at least equal or greater than that of morphine on a weight basis.

### Chemical synthesis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Morphine&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Chemical synthesis")]

Main article: [Morphine total synthesis](/wiki/Morphine_total_synthesis "Morphine total synthesis")

The first [morphine total synthesis](/wiki/Morphine_total_synthesis "Morphine total synthesis"), devised by [Marshall D. Gates, Jr.](/wiki/Marshall_D._Gates,_Jr. "Marshall D. Gates, Jr.") in 1952, remains a widely used example of [total synthesis](/wiki/Total_synthesis "Total synthesis").[[118]](#cite_note-118) Several other syntheses were reported, notably by the research groups of Rice,[[119]](#cite_note-119) Evans,[[120]](#cite_note-120) Fuchs,[[121]](#cite_note-121) Parker,[[122]](#cite_note-122) Overman,[[123]](#cite_note-123) Mulzer-Trauner,[[124]](#cite_note-124) White,[[125]](#cite_note-125) Taber,[[126]](#cite_note-126) Trost,[[127]](#cite_note-127) Fukuyama,[[128]](#cite_note-128) Guillou,[[129]](#cite_note-129) and Stork.[[130]](#cite_note-130) Because of the stereochemical complexity and consequent synthetic challenge presented by this [polycyclic](/wiki/Polycyclic_compound "Polycyclic compound") structure, Michael Freemantle has expressed the view that it is "highly unlikely" that a chemical synthesis will ever be cost-effective such that it could compete with the cost of producing morphine from the opium poppy.[[131]](#cite_note-131)
